retrieved_docs.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: New lesions on chest imaging should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary team involving chest specialists, surgeons, and radiologists to decide the next best management. [3] A lung biopsy is required when there is a need to provide a specific diagnosis. Indications for PTLB according to the BTS guidelines (in near-verbatim language) are as follows: [3] New or enlarging mass or solitary nodule on chest radiography that is not amenable to diagnosis by CT or bronchoscopy shows it is unlikely to be accessible by bronchoscopy. Multiple nodules in a patient with no previous history of malignancy, who have had a prolonged remission or more than one primary malignancy. Persistent focal infiltrates for which no diagnosis has been made by sputum, or bronchoscopy, blood culture, serology. Hilar mass.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Failure to obtain informed consent is a contraindication for the procedure according to the guidelines, and an alternative diagnostic modality must be considered. The previous history of pneumonectomy is a contraindication. However, if the lesion to be biopsied is pleural based, then the procedure can be attempted as long as the lung tissue is not traversed. If a vascular lesion or AVM is suspected on CT, the procedure should not be considered, and a multidisciplinary team meeting should be held to decide the next step in management. For patients on mechanical ventilation, lung biopsy may not be feasible due to limited access. Despite pre-procedure counseling, some patients may be uncooperative and move during the procedure. Hence, another diagnostic modality must be considered in these cases. Abnormal lung function or coagulation indices during the pre-procedure evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: CT Guided Percutaneous Lung Biopsy : Preferably, under moderate sedation, images via CT or CT fluoroscopy are taken, and the route of access is planned. At times a coaxial technique may be used to minimize the number of pleural punctures. After confirmation of position with CT images, either FNA or core biopsy is taken, and the sample is immediately handed over to either a cytopathologist or cytotechnician. [13] Other advanced techniques like the trans-sternal approach and the transvenous approach may be used. retrieved_docs.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Transthoracic needle biopsy (TTNB) is indicated for evaluating an undiagnosed pulmonary nodule or mass depending on the pretest probability of cancer and the presence or absence of metastasis. TTNB is also useful for the evaluation of mediastinal masses, hilar masses that are undiagnosed post bronchoscopy, and also for the diagnoses of infective diseases presenting as nodules or consolidation. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment -- Additional Procedural Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Time management: The time the needle lies across the pleural space is indirectly proportional to the risk of complications. Streamline the procedure to avoid unnecessary interruptions, including prolonged duration of ROSE, improper imaging techniques, and unnecessary additional imaging. Ensure personnel know their roles and that anyone entering the CT room is already equipped with the proper radiation safety equipment.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Contraindications -- Noncooperative, Nontrained, or Over-Sedated Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Lung tumors exhibit significant mobility in various directions, regardless of their anatomical location. [14] PLLBs are still performed without real-time imaging guidance in hospitals lacking CT-fluoroscopy technology. A person who cannot remain still or who is not trained to breathe in a controlled fashion for the time needed to perform the biopsy reduces the odds of successful tissue retrieval and increases the odds of complications. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Endobronchial Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Careful patient selection for the procedure should be undertaken by clinicians themselves experienced in performing EBUS. Review of the clinical history and relevant pre-procedure investigations such as computed tomography (CT) and positron-electron tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT) is important as the lesion size and the nodal station may suggest to the operator that a different diagnostic test would be preferable. These include ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration, endoscopic ultrasound, mediastinoscopy, or VATS.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with FEV, less than 35% predicted on recent spirometry should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary team with a radiologist and pulmonologist as a minimum should be consulted to assess the risk vs. benefit of going ahead with the procedure. Recent as well as previous radiographs must be available to decide if the procedure is beneficial. In the case of a bronchoscopic biopsy, the procedure should not be performed within six weeks of myocardial infarction. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment -- Post-Biopsy Management and Discharge
retrieved_docs.content: The decision to discharge a patient from the hospital, rather than admitting them for observation or treatment, is based on several factors, including the patient's condition, ability to be observed at home, and home location. Patients with a pneumothorax should be admitted or observed unless pneumothorax stability despite light activity is demonstrated, in which case the patient is only discharged if the following are present: The patient's prebiopsy testing parameters indicate that they are likely to survive subsequent pneumothorax growth while awaiting emergency responders to provide chest decompression. A responsible individual is willing to stay the night with the patient. The patient lives within 20 minutes of the medical facility or for ambulance arrival after a 911 call. Delayed pneumothorax necessitating chest tube insertion has occurred as late as 2 weeks after PLLB. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2396
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus and bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy are both classified as diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus procedures. These diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus interventions are performed directly on the tracheobronchial structure, which serves as the procedural site.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy is classified as a specific type of diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus. This diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus, in turn, is one of the procedures encompassed by the broader category Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus, which itself is a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus. The Tracheobronchial structure is identified as the direct anatomic site of the diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus, and it is continuous with the organ referred to as plíce (the lungs). Accordingly, Bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy also has plíce listed as its procedure site, indicating that the lungs are directly involved in the intervention. The term bronchy is related to Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus and is linked to plíce, suggesting that bronchy represents a bronchoscopy‑related concept that pertains to lung anatomy. In imaging, computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate records its findings at the site of plíce, reinforcing the clinical importance of lung assessment in both diagnostic and therapeutic contexts. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical and anatomical framework in which bronchoscopy‑based lung biopsies are performed on the tracheobronchial tree and lungs, are categorized within comprehensive lung and bronchus diagnostic procedures, and are complemented by chest CT imaging that evaluates pulmonary pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1308
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy is classified as a diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of the bronchus, and the tracheobronchial structure is identified as the direct anatomic site where this diagnostic procedure is performed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific types of chest CT scans. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material, as well as diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, represent narrower sub‑categories within the broader chest CT imaging modality. In addition, computed tomography of the chest is used as a finding method for detecting miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure of chest CT techniques and illustrate how particular CT protocols are applied for detailed diagnostic evaluation of thoracic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: bronchoscopy as the overarching procedural category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic or therapeutic bronchoscopic approaches, aligning procedural choice with the intended clinical goal and the specific anatomic site involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2349
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus are classified as a specific subset within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus, indicating that these lung‑ and bronchus‑focused diagnostic interventions fall under the larger group of related diagnostic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1099
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy is a specific form of diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of the bronchus, and it is performed directly on the tracheobronchial structure. This procedure is encompassed within the broader category of trachea and bronchus operations, a group that also includes other interventions such as تنبيب الرغامى (tracheal intubation). Together, these relationships clarify that the tracheobronchial structure serves as the direct site for diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of the bronchus, while both bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy and tracheal intubation are part of the larger set of trachea and bronchus operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2537
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Intervention Terminology is a collection of thoracic‑related concepts and procedures. It includes the concept تسريب, the diagnostic imaging procedure الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray), the clinical examination بضع الصدر, and the therapeutic approach عملية صدرية (thoracic surgery). In addition, the specific interventional procedure bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus is also classified within CTRP Intervention Terminology, illustrating that this terminology set encompasses both diagnostic and operative interventions used in the assessment and management of chest diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1725
retrieved_docs.content: Inspection (procedure) is a diagnostic category that incorporates several specific methods for visual and functional assessment of internal organs. Among its techniques are mediastinoscopy and thoracoscopy performed with ventilation of the lung, which allow direct visualization and sampling of mediastinal and pleural structures while maintaining adequate respiratory support. Another method included under inspection is the combination of paracentesis and endoscopic examination of the ileum, enabling fluid analysis together with direct endoscopic evaluation of the distal small intestine. In addition, inspection procedures serve as the method of choice for other bowel diagnostic procedures, encompassing a range of endoscopic and minimally invasive approaches used to assess gastrointestinal pathology. Together, these methods provide clinicians with essential information for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 94
retrieved_docs.content: Within the respiratory tract hierarchy (عملية في المجرى التنفسي) are diagnostic bronchoscopy and bronchial biopsy, broader diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus, excision or destruction of lung/bronchial lesions, other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum, and unspecified therapeutic respiratory procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالسبيل التنفسي، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). Therapeutic endoscopic techniques employ a defined removal technique for the airway: endoscopic removal of an intraluminal foreign body from the trachea, fiberoptic bronchoscopic removal of foreign body, and removal of an intraluminal foreign body from the bronchus by bronchoscopy.
